chapter: Ch 4 · Presentation & Disclosures Based Accounting Standards

questions.stem: Under AS 1, if the fundamental accounting assumptions of going concern, consistency and accrual are followed in preparing the financial statements:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U1 — fundamental accounting assumptions

readLink.label: Read: AS 1 — Disclosure of Accounting Policies →

options.text: Each must be affirmatively disclosed in the policy note
options.explanation: AS 1 requires disclosure only when an assumption is NOT followed — silence signals compliance.

options.text: No specific disclosure of that fact is required
options.explanation: The assumptions are presumed; only a DEPARTURE must be disclosed.

options.text: The auditor must certify compliance separately
options.explanation: AS 1 imposes no separate certification; it is a preparer's disclosure standard.

options.text: Only going concern must be affirmatively confirmed
options.explanation: No assumption needs affirmative confirmation; the rule is one-directional.

questions.stem: AS 1 names three major considerations governing the selection of accounting policies. They are:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U1 — considerations in selection of policies

readLink.label: Read: AS 1 — Disclosure of Accounting Policies →

options.text: Prudence, substance over form and materiality
options.explanation: The master test is true and fair presentation, served by these three considerations.

options.text: Timeliness, neutrality and completeness
options.explanation: These traits appear in the Framework's reliability cluster, not in AS 1's list.

options.text: Relevance, reliability and comparability
options.explanation: Those are qualitative characteristics from the Framework, not AS 1's selection trio.

options.text: Going concern, consistency and accrual
options.explanation: Those are the fundamental accounting assumptions, not the selection considerations.

questions.stem: A company changes an accounting policy during the year. The change has a material effect whose amount cannot be fully quantified. AS 1 requires:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U1 — disclosure of changes in policies

readLink.label: Read: AS 1 — Disclosure of Accounting Policies →

options.text: Withdrawal of the change until the effect can be measured
options.explanation: AS 1 never forces a policy to be reversed; it regulates the disclosure.

options.text: No disclosure, since the amount is not ascertainable
options.explanation: Inability to quantify does not excuse disclosure — the fact of non-ascertainability itself must be disclosed.

options.text: Disclosure only in the year the effect becomes measurable
options.explanation: Disclosure belongs to the period of the change, not a later period.

options.text: Disclosure of the change and, to the extent ascertainable, the amount — and where not ascertainable, disclosure of that fact
options.explanation: Both the change and the money effect (or the inability to quantify it) must be stated.

questions.stem: A company adopts an inappropriate accounting treatment but describes it fully and candidly in its accounting-policy note. Under AS 1:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U1 — disclosure not a remedy

readLink.label: Read: AS 1 — Disclosure of Accounting Policies →

options.text: The treatment becomes acceptable because users are informed
options.explanation: Transparency does not convert a wrong treatment into a right one.

options.text: The treatment remains wrong — disclosure does not remedy a wrong or inappropriate treatment
options.explanation: AS 1 says expressly that disclosure is no substitute for correct accounting.

options.text: The treatment is acceptable if the auditor concurs
options.explanation: Auditor concurrence cannot cure a treatment AS 1 labels inappropriate.

options.text: The treatment is acceptable if applied consistently
options.explanation: Consistent error is still error; consistency is no cure.

questions.stem: Regarding the manner of disclosing accounting policies, AS 1 requires that:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U1 — manner of disclosure

readLink.label: Read: AS 1 — Disclosure of Accounting Policies →

options.text: Policies be disclosed wherever the related line item appears
options.explanation: That is the scattering AS 1 exists to prevent — one place, not many.

options.text: Policies be disclosed only for items material to the balance sheet
options.explanation: The requirement covers all significant policies, not a balance-sheet subset.

options.text: Policies be filed with the Registrar but not printed in the statements
options.explanation: The disclosure forms part of the financial statements themselves.

options.text: All significant accounting policies be disclosed in one place, forming part of the financial statements
options.explanation: Scattered policy notes defeat the reader; AS 1 centralises them.

questions.stem: At 31 March a company holds: cash on hand ₹40,000; demand deposits ₹1,10,000; a treasury bill acquired on 15 February maturing 30 April ₹50,000; a bank fixed deposit maturing in nine months ₹1,00,000; listed equity shares ₹75,000. Cash and cash equivalents per AS 3 total:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U2 — cash and cash equivalents

options.explanation: Includes the nine-month fixed deposit, which fails the ~three-month maturity test.
Includes the equity shares — price risk is not 'insignificant risk of change in value'.

Cash 40,000 + demand deposits 1,10,000 + T-bill 50,000 (≈2.5-month original maturity, insignificant risk). The 9-month FD fails the short-maturity test; equity has no fixed maturity and real price risk.
Drops the treasury bill, which qualifies: short maturity from acquisition and negligible value risk.

questions.stem: For an enterprise other than a financial enterprise, AS 3 classifies interest and dividends as:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U2 — interest and dividends

options.text: Interest received — operating; dividends paid — investing
options.explanation: Neither leg matches AS 3's classification for non-financial enterprises.

options.text: All interest and dividends — operating
options.explanation: That is the financial-enterprise treatment (except dividends paid), not the general rule.

options.text: Interest paid — operating; dividends received — financing
options.explanation: Reversed: paid items are financing, received items are investing.

options.text: Interest and dividends received — investing; interest paid and dividends paid — financing
options.explanation: Returns ON investments come in as investing; the cost of capital goes out as financing.

questions.stem: For a financial enterprise (say, a bank), cash flows from interest paid, and interest and dividends received, are classified as:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U2 — financial enterprises

options.text: Investing activities
options.explanation: That is the non-financial treatment of interest/dividends received.

options.text: Financing activities
options.explanation: Only dividends PAID are financing for a financial enterprise.

options.text: Operating activities
options.explanation: Lending and investing IS the principal revenue-producing activity of a financial enterprise; dividends paid remain financing.

options.text: Split equally between operating and investing
options.explanation: AS 3 prescribes no such apportionment.

questions.stem: Under AS 3, cash flows arising from taxes on income should be:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U2 — taxes on income

options.text: Apportioned between the three activities in the ratio of their gross flows
options.explanation: AS 3 prescribes no ratio apportionment for taxes.

options.text: Classified as operating, unless specifically identifiable with financing or investing activities
options.explanation: Operating is the default; a tax that rides a specific investing/financing transaction rides with it.

options.text: Always classified as operating without exception
options.explanation: The default yields where the tax is specifically identifiable with an investing or financing flow.

options.text: Excluded from the statement as a non-cash item
options.explanation: Tax paid is emphatically a cash flow.

questions.stem: A company issues equity shares to the vendor of a machine in full settlement. In the cash flow statement this is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U2 — non-cash transactions

options.text: Excluded from the statement and disclosed elsewhere in the financial statements
options.explanation: No cash moved — investing and financing transactions without cash flows are disclosed outside the statement.

options.text: Shown as an investing outflow and a financing inflow of equal amount
options.explanation: Grossing up a cash-less exchange fabricates flows that never happened.

options.text: Shown as a financing outflow only
options.explanation: The share issue is not an outflow, and no cash moved either way.

options.text: Shown as an operating flow since it settles a supplier
options.explanation: A machine vendor is not an operating supplier, and no cash flowed.

questions.stem: Which statement about the obligation to present a cash flow statement is correct?
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U2 + Ch 3 — applicability of AS 3

options.text: Only listed companies present cash flow statements
options.explanation: Unlisted companies outside the s.2(40) carve-outs must also present one.

options.text: All companies without exception must present a cash flow statement
options.explanation: OPCs, small companies and dormant companies are excluded by the s.2(40) definition of financial statements.

options.text: Every SMC is exempt from preparing a cash flow statement under the AS Rules
options.explanation: AS 3 is not on the SMC exemption list — the company-law s.2(40) proviso, not the AS regime, gives the escape.

options.text: An SMC is not exempted from AS 3 by the AS Rules; a company escapes the cash flow statement only via s.2(40) of the Companies Act (OPC, small company, dormant company)
options.explanation: AS 3 is outside the SMC exemption list; the company-law carve-out in s.2(40) is what excuses OPCs, small and dormant companies.

questions.stem: Under AS 17, a business segment is a distinguishable component of an enterprise:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U3 — business segment definition

options.text: Operating from a distinct geographical location
options.explanation: That describes a geographical segment; a business segment is defined by product/service risks and returns.

options.text: Contributing at least 10% of enterprise revenue
options.explanation: 10% decides REPORTABILITY, not whether something is a segment.

options.text: Constituted as a separate legal entity within the group
options.explanation: Segments are components of one enterprise; separate incorporation is irrelevant.

options.text: Providing an individual product or service (or related group) that is subject to risks and returns different from other business segments
options.explanation: Differing risks and returns — not size, location or legal form — is the defining test.

questions.stem: Whether the primary segment reporting format is business or geographical is determined by:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U3 — primary and secondary formats

options.text: A free choice of management, reviewed annually
options.explanation: The format follows the dominant risk source, not managerial preference.

options.text: Whichever format has more segments crossing 10%
options.explanation: Threshold arithmetic does not decide the primary/secondary split.

options.text: The format used by the largest competitor
options.explanation: Comparability with rivals is not the AS 17 test.

options.text: The dominant source and nature of the enterprise's risks and returns, normally evidenced by its internal organisation and reporting structure
options.explanation: Risks and returns dominate; the internal structure is the usual evidence of which dimension dominates.

questions.stem: Which item is INCLUDED in segment revenue under AS 17?
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U3 — segment revenue definition

options.text: Interest and dividend income of a non-financial segment
options.explanation: Interest/dividend income is excluded unless the segment's operations are primarily financial.

options.text: The enterprise's share of inter-segment transfers of the segment
options.explanation: Segment revenue is external sales PLUS inter-segment transfers attributable to the segment.

options.text: Gains on sale of investments held centrally
options.explanation: Central investment gains are not attributable segment revenue.

options.text: Extraordinary items arising in the segment
options.explanation: Extraordinary items are excluded from segment revenue.

questions.stem: A segment crossed the 10% thresholds last year but fails all three this year. Management judges it of continuing significance. AS 17 requires that the segment:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U3 — segments reportable in prior period

options.text: Be reported only in the secondary format
options.explanation: The carry-forward keeps it in the primary format, not a demoted one.

options.text: Be merged into the largest reportable segment
options.explanation: AS 17 has no forced-merger rule for a lapsed segment.

options.text: Be dropped from segment reporting immediately
options.explanation: Dropping it would break comparability — continuing significance keeps it in.

options.text: Continue to be reported as a reportable segment this year
options.explanation: Once judged of continuing significance, last year's reportable segment stays reportable despite failing the tests.

questions.stem: Which of the following is a related party of Reliance-style reporting enterprise X Ltd under AS 18?
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U4 — who is related / deemed not related

options.text: The managing director's spouse
options.explanation: KMP and their relatives (spouse, son, daughter, brother, sister, father, mother) are related parties.

options.text: A company sharing one common non-executive director with X, who cannot affect mutual-dealing policies
options.explanation: A common director alone is deemed NOT to create a related-party relationship.

options.text: A supplier from whom X sources 60% of raw material, with no other connection
options.explanation: Economic dependence alone does not create a related party — deemed not related.

options.text: The public utility supplying X's electricity
options.explanation: Utilities and government departments in normal dealings are deemed not related.

questions.stem: Under AS 18, 'control' means:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U4 — control definition

options.text: Being the largest single shareholder regardless of percentage
options.explanation: Relative size confers nothing without a control limb being met.

options.text: Owning any equity stake exceeding 20% of voting power
options.explanation: 20% raises a presumption of significant influence, not control.

options.text: Owning more than half the voting power, or controlling board composition, or holding a substantial interest in voting power with power to direct financial and operating policies
options.explanation: All three limbs are alternative routes to control.

options.text: Having a right to appoint at least one director
options.explanation: One seat is neither board control nor policy-direction power.

questions.stem: An investor holds 20% of the voting power of an enterprise. Under AS 18 this:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U4 — significant influence

options.text: Automatically establishes control
options.explanation: Control needs >50% votes, board control, or substantial interest plus policy power.

options.text: Is irrelevant unless the investor also has board representation
options.explanation: Board seats are evidence, not a precondition — the 20% presumption stands alone.

options.text: Creates a related-party relationship only if transactions occur
options.explanation: The relationship exists independent of whether transactions occurred (disclosure differs).

options.text: Raises a presumption of significant influence, rebuttable by circumstances
options.explanation: 20% or more presumes participation in policy decisions unless shown otherwise.

questions.stem: Which disclosure exemption does AS 18 actually give?
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U4 — exemptions from disclosure

options.text: A private company need not disclose transactions with its holding company
options.explanation: No such blanket private-company exemption exists.

options.text: A state-controlled enterprise need not disclose related-party transactions with other state-controlled enterprises
options.explanation: Also exempt: disclosures conflicting with statutory/regulatory confidentiality, and intra-group items eliminated in consolidation.

options.text: Remuneration to key management personnel need not be disclosed
options.explanation: KMP remuneration is itself a disclosable related-party transaction.

options.text: Transactions below ₹1 lakh need not be disclosed
options.explanation: AS 18 sets no monetary de-minimis threshold.

questions.topic: Ch 4 · AS 18 · Control with no transactions
questions.stem: H Ltd controls S Ltd throughout the year but had no transactions with it. In S Ltd's financial statements:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U4 — disclosure where control exists

options.text: A nil-transactions certificate replaces disclosure
options.explanation: AS 18 knows no such certificate.

options.text: The name of H Ltd and the nature of the control relationship must still be disclosed
options.explanation: Where control exists, relationship disclosure is unconditional — transactions or none.

options.text: No disclosure is needed since there were no transactions
options.explanation: Control relationships are disclosed even in a transaction-free year.

options.text: Disclosure is needed only in H Ltd's consolidated statements
options.explanation: Each reporting enterprise's separate statements carry the disclosure.

questions.stem: Under AS 18, items of a similar nature with related parties:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U4 — aggregation of similar items

options.text: May be netted against unrelated-party transactions of the same type
options.explanation: Netting related against unrelated dealings hides exactly what AS 18 exposes.

options.text: May be disclosed in aggregate by type of related party, unless separate disclosure is necessary to understand the effects on the financial statements
options.explanation: Aggregation is the default convenience; understanding is its limit.

options.text: Must always be disclosed transaction by transaction
options.explanation: Item-by-item listing is not required where aggregation communicates adequately.

options.text: Need not be disclosed if individually immaterial
options.explanation: Similar immaterial items can aggregate into materiality — the test is on the whole.

questions.stem: AS 20 requires an enterprise to present:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U5 — presentation of EPS

options.text: Basic EPS on the face and diluted EPS in the notes
options.explanation: Both figures belong on the face with equal prominence.

options.text: Basic and diluted EPS on the face of the statement of profit and loss, even when the amounts are negative
options.explanation: Face-of-P&L presentation for both figures, with equal prominence — a loss per share is still presented.

options.text: EPS only when the figure is positive
options.explanation: Negative EPS (loss per share) is presented too.

options.text: EPS only in consolidated financial statements
options.explanation: AS 20 applies to the separate statements as well.

questions.stem: A company (year ending 31 March) had 90,000 equity shares on 1 April, issued 30,000 shares for cash on 1 July, and bought back 12,000 shares on 1 January. The weighted average number of shares for basic EPS is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U5 — weighted average number of shares

options.explanation: That is the closing count — the year-end snapshot, not the time-weighted average.
That is the peak count between July and December, unweighted.
Mis-weights the periods; work month-blocks: 3, 6 and 3 twelfths.

questions.stem: Net profit for the year is ₹18,00,000. The company has ₹20,00,000 of 10% cumulative preference capital (dividend not declared this year) and 4,00,000 equity shares. Basic EPS is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U5 — earnings attributable to equity

options.explanation: Deducts two years of preference dividend; only the current period's is deducted.
Cumulative preference dividend of ₹2,00,000 is deducted whether or not declared: 16,00,000 ÷ 4,00,000.
Uses ₹20,00,000 of profit or 3,60,000 shares — check both inputs.
Skipping the deduction because the dividend was not declared — cumulative dividends are deducted regardless.

questions.stem: A bonus issue made during the current year is treated, for EPS purposes, as if it had occurred:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U5 — bonus issues and splits

options.text: At the end of the year, affecting next year only
options.explanation: Deferral would misstate both years' EPS.

options.text: At the beginning of the earliest period reported — restating comparatives
options.explanation: No resources came in, so all periods are restated for comparability.

options.text: At the start of the current year, leaving comparatives untouched
options.explanation: Comparatives are restated too — otherwise the trend is broken.

options.text: On the actual date of allotment, time-weighted like a cash issue
options.explanation: Time-weighting is for issues that change resources; a bonus changes none.

questions.stem: A company has 50,000 employee options outstanding with an exercise price of ₹60; the average fair value of a share during the year is ₹75. For diluted EPS the options add to the denominator:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U5 — options and warrants in diluted EPS

options.text: 12,500 shares
options.explanation: Divides the ₹15 discount by the ₹60 exercise price instead of the ₹75 fair value.

options.text: 50,000 shares
options.explanation: The full option count ignores that ₹60 of value arrives per share exercised.

options.text: 40,000 shares
options.explanation: That is the 'paid-for' portion (60/75 × 50,000) — the part that does NOT dilute.

options.text: 10,000 shares
options.explanation: Only the bonus element dilutes: 50,000 × (75 − 60)/75 = 10,000 shares issued for no consideration.

questions.stem: An enterprise reports a net LOSS for the year and has convertible debentures outstanding. On conversion the loss per share would fall. For diluted EPS the potential shares are:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U5 — dilutive vs anti-dilutive

options.text: Included at half weight
options.explanation: AS 20 has no half-weight rule.

options.text: Excluded — reducing a loss per share is anti-dilutive
options.explanation: Dilution means a WORSE figure for equity holders; improving the loss per share fails the test, so diluted EPS equals basic.

options.text: Included — every potential share enters diluted EPS
options.explanation: Only dilutive potential shares enter; anti-dilutive ones are ignored.

options.text: Excluded only if conversion is more than a year away
options.explanation: Time-to-conversion is not the test; the dilution direction is.

questions.stem: Under AS 24, a discontinuing operation must satisfy which combination of conditions?
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U6 — discontinuing operation definition

options.text: Any disposal of assets exceeding 10% of total assets
options.explanation: Size of asset disposal is not the test; the three limbs are.

options.text: Any activity making losses for three consecutive years
options.explanation: Persistent losses are not the trigger; a single-plan exit is.

options.text: Disposal pursuant to a single plan (in entirety, piecemeal, or by abandonment) + a separate major line of business or geographical area + distinguishable operationally and for financial reporting
options.explanation: All three limbs are cumulative.

options.text: Any operation reported as a segment under AS 17
options.explanation: Segment status helps limb (c) but does not by itself create a discontinuing operation.

questions.stem: The initial disclosure event under AS 24 is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U6 — initial disclosure event

options.text: The LATER of the binding sale agreement and the board announcement
options.explanation: Waiting for both would defeat the early-warning purpose — it is the earlier.

options.text: The start of the financial year in which management first considers an exit
options.explanation: Mere consideration, without agreement or approved-and-announced plan, triggers nothing.

options.text: The EARLIER of a binding sale agreement for substantially all the component's assets, and board approval PLUS announcement of a detailed formal plan
options.explanation: Whichever of the two happens first starts the disclosure.

options.text: The date the last asset of the component is sold
options.explanation: That is the END of the discontinuance, not the disclosure trigger.

questions.stem: On recognition and measurement, AS 24:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U6 — recognition and measurement

options.text: Requires a provision for the entire future operating losses of the operation
options.explanation: Future operating losses are not provided for merely because discontinuance is planned.

options.text: Freezes depreciation on the component's assets from the initial disclosure event
options.explanation: No such freeze exists; normal standards keep applying.

options.text: Establishes no new principles — provisions, impairments and employee terminations follow the other applicable standards
options.explanation: AS 24 is a presentation/disclosure standard riding on AS 29, AS 28, AS 15 etc.

options.text: Requires assets of the discontinuing operation to be written down to net realisable value
options.explanation: Any write-down comes from AS 28's impairment rules, not from AS 24 itself.

questions.topic: Ch 4 · AS 24 · What does not qualify
questions.stem: Which of the following is a discontinuing operation under AS 24?
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U6 — activities that are not discontinuing operations

options.text: Selling the entire fertiliser division — a separate major line of business — under a board-approved single plan, by demerger
options.explanation: Single plan + major line + operationally distinguishable: all three limbs met.

options.text: Shifting production of a product from one plant to another
options.explanation: Relocation discontinues nothing — the line of business continues.

options.text: Closing two of forty retail branches to cut costs
options.explanation: A minor pruning is restructuring, not exit from a separate major line.

options.text: Gradually phasing out an ageing product model as demand declines
options.explanation: Gradual evolution without a single plan to exit a major line is not a discontinuing operation.

questions.stem: The results of a discontinuing operation are presented:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U6 — presentation and disclosure

options.text: As an extraordinary item below the line
options.explanation: AS 24 expressly keeps discontinuing operations out of extraordinary items.

options.text: Only in the year the discontinuance completes
options.explanation: Disclosure runs every period from the initial disclosure event until completion.

options.text: Within ordinary activities, separately disclosed — a discontinuing operation is not an extraordinary item, and comparatives are restated to segregate it
options.explanation: Discontinuance is a business decision, squarely ordinary; disclosure, not exile, is the treatment.

options.text: Net of tax as a single line with no further analysis
options.explanation: Revenue, expenses, pre-tax result AND tax are disclosed, with cash flows by activity.

questions.stem: Which statement about the scope of AS 25 is correct?
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U7 — objective and scope

options.text: AS 25 applies only when a complete set of statements is presented
options.explanation: It governs condensed interim statements too — that is its main use.

options.text: AS 25 applies only to half-yearly reports
options.explanation: Any period shorter than a full financial year is an interim period.

options.text: AS 25 requires every listed company to publish quarterly reports
options.explanation: The listing regulations impose that; AS 25 itself mandates no reporter.

options.text: AS 25 does not mandate who must publish interim reports or how often — but any enterprise required to, or electing to, present one must comply with it
options.explanation: Statute and regulators (e.g. SEBI) pick the reporters; AS 25 disciplines the report.

questions.stem: The minimum components of a condensed interim financial report are:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U7 — minimum components

options.text: Balance sheet and profit and loss only
options.explanation: The condensed cash flow statement and selected notes are also mandatory.

options.text: Condensed balance sheet, condensed statement of profit and loss, condensed cash flow statement, and selected explanatory notes
options.explanation: Four components — each retaining the headings and subtotals of the latest annual statements.

options.text: A complete set of annual-style statements
options.explanation: A complete set is permitted, but the MINIMUM is the condensed four.

options.text: A directors' report with summarised accounts
options.explanation: Directors' reports are company-law documents, not AS 25 components.

questions.stem: In a half-yearly interim report as at 30 September 2025, the comparative balance sheet presented is as at:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U7 — periods to be presented

options.text: No comparative balance sheet is required
options.explanation: A comparative is required — as at the preceding full year end.

options.text: 31 March 2025 — the end of the immediately preceding full financial year
options.explanation: Balance sheets compare interim date against the last annual date; only the P&L compares like interim periods.

options.text: 30 September 2024 — the comparable interim date
options.explanation: Comparable-interim comparison is the P&L rule; the balance sheet uses the preceding year end.

options.text: 31 March 2024 — two annual dates back
options.explanation: One preceding annual date, not two.

questions.stem: An enterprise earns most of its revenue in the festival quarter. In its interim reports, seasonal revenue:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U7 — seasonal revenues

options.text: Is recognised when it occurs — not anticipated in lean quarters nor deferred out of fat ones, if that treatment would be improper at year end
options.explanation: The year-end recognisability test governs interim treatment; the seasonality is instead explained in the notes.

options.text: Is spread evenly over the four quarters
options.explanation: Smoothing fabricates revenue in quarters that earned none.

options.text: Is deferred until the annual statements
options.explanation: Interim statements report the period's actual activity.

options.text: Is recognised only to the extent of the annual budget proportion
options.explanation: Budgets do not drive recognition.

questions.stem: Estimated annual income is ₹8,00,000, taxed at 30% on the first ₹5,00,000 and 40% on the balance. The weighted average annual effective tax rate for interim reporting is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U7 — income tax in interim periods

options.explanation: Using the marginal slab overtaxes the first ₹5,00,000.
Tax = 1,50,000 + 1,20,000 = ₹2,70,000 on ₹8,00,000 → 33.75%, applied to each interim period's income.
The unweighted mean of the two rates ignores the slab widths.
Using only the lower slab ignores the ₹3,00,000 taxed at 40%.

questions.stem: For interim reporting, AS 25 requires materiality to be assessed in relation to:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U7 — materiality

options.text: The expected full-year results
options.explanation: Assessing against annual figures would bury interim-material items.

options.text: The previous year's annual financial statements
options.explanation: History does not set the current quarter's materiality.

options.text: The interim period's financial data itself
options.explanation: A small annual number can be material to one quarter — the interim data is the yardstick.

options.text: A fixed 5% of turnover threshold
options.explanation: AS 25 sets no fixed percentage.

questions.stem: An estimate reported in an earlier interim period changes materially during the FINAL interim period, and the enterprise publishes no separate report for that period. AS 25 requires:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U7 — disclosure in annual statements

options.text: Disclosure of the nature and amount of the change in a note to the annual financial statements
options.explanation: The annual notes catch what the unpublished final interim period would have shown.

options.text: No disclosure, as the annual statements supersede interim data
options.explanation: The change must still be disclosed — nature and amount.

options.text: A special-purpose report for the final interim period
options.explanation: No separate report is forced; the annual notes carry the disclosure.

options.text: Restatement of the earlier interim reports
options.explanation: Changes in estimate are never restated retrospectively.

questions.topic: Ch 4 · AS 3 · Case: Vindhya Engineering (indirect method)
questions.case: Vindhya Engineering Ltd (not a financial enterprise) reports for the year ended 31 March: net profit before tax ₹6,40,000 after charging depreciation ₹1,50,000 and interest expense ₹60,000, and after crediting profit of ₹40,000 on sale of a machine and interest income of ₹30,000. During the year: trade receivables rose ₹70,000, inventory fell ₹45,000, trade payables rose ₹25,000; income tax paid ₹1,80,000. The machine (carrying amount ₹1,50,000) sold for ₹1,90,000; new machinery bought ₹4,00,000; long-term investments bought ₹1,20,000; interest received in cash ₹30,000. Equity shares issued for cash ₹3,00,000; debentures repaid ₹2,00,000; interest paid in cash ₹60,000; dividend paid ₹90,000. Cash and cash equivalents on 1 April were ₹1,25,000.

questions.stem: Net cash from operating activities is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U2 — indirect method

options.explanation: Leaves interest income inside operating — it belongs to investing.
Stops at operating profit before working-capital changes and tax.
Fails to add back interest expense before reclassifying it to financing.

questions.stem: Net cash used in investing activities is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U2 — investing activities

options.text: ₹3,30,000 outflow
options.explanation: Leaves interest received in operating; for a non-financial company it is investing.

options.text: ₹2,60,000 outflow
options.explanation: Adds the ₹40,000 profit on sale again — the proceeds already contain it.

options.text: ₹3,40,000 outflow
options.explanation: Uses the machine's carrying amount ₹1,50,000 instead of sale proceeds ₹1,90,000.

options.text: ₹3,00,000 outflow
options.explanation: −4,00,000 (machinery) + 1,90,000 (sale proceeds) − 1,20,000 (investments) + 30,000 (interest received) = −3,00,000.

questions.stem: Net cash from/used in financing activities is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U2 — financing activities

options.text: ₹1,40,000 outflow
options.explanation: Wrongly drags the ₹90,000 dividend AND tax paid into financing.

options.text: ₹90,000 outflow
options.explanation: Counts only the dividend and ignores the share issue, repayment and interest.

options.text: ₹50,000 outflow
₹10,000 inflow
options.explanation: Omits interest paid — for a non-financial company it is a financing outflow.

questions.stem: Cash and cash equivalents at 31 March are:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U2 — reconciliation of cash

options.explanation: That is the opening balance.
Adds the increase twice or forgets to net the outflows.
That is the net INCREASE for the year, not the closing balance.

questions.topic: Ch 4 · AS 17 · Case: Panchtatva Industries (reportable segments)
questions.case: Panchtatva Industries Ltd has five segments (₹ in lakh). External revenue: A 500, B 200, C 90, D 60, E 50 (total 900). Inter-segment revenue: A 100, C 10, D 40 (total 150). Segment results: A +90, B (140), C +25, D (10), E +12. Segment assets: A 400, B 300, C 80, D 150, E 70 (total 1,000).

questions.stem: Applying the revenue test alone (10% of combined internal and external revenue of all segments), the segments that qualify are:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U3 — revenue test

options.text: A, B and C
options.explanation: C totals 100 (90 + 10) — just below the ₹105 lakh threshold.

options.text: A only
options.explanation: B's ₹200 lakh clears the ₹105 lakh threshold comfortably.

options.text: A, B and D
options.explanation: D totals 100 (60 + 40) — below the ₹105 lakh threshold.

options.text: A and B only
options.explanation: Threshold = 10% of 1,050 = ₹105 lakh. A: 600 ✓, B: 200 ✓; C: 100, D: 100 and E: 50 all fall short.

questions.stem: For the result test, the 10% threshold is applied to:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U3 — result test

options.text: ₹23 lakh — the net combined result of all segments
options.explanation: Netting profits against losses is exactly what the test avoids.

options.text: ₹127 lakh — the combined profit of profit-making segments
options.explanation: The rule picks the GREATER absolute pool; 150 > 127.

options.text: ₹150 lakh — the combined loss of loss-making segments, being greater in absolute amount than the combined ₹127 lakh profit
options.explanation: Profits: 90 + 25 + 12 = 127; losses: 140 + 10 = 150. Test against |−150| → threshold ₹15 lakh.

options.text: ₹277 lakh — the sum of absolute results of all segments
options.explanation: The pools are compared, not added together.

questions.stem: After applying all three 10% tests and the 75% check, the reportable segments are:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U3 — combining the tests with the 75% floor

options.text: All five segments
options.explanation: E fails all three tests and the 75% floor is already met without it.

options.text: A and B only
options.explanation: C clears the result test (25 ≥ 15) and D clears the asset test (150 ≥ 100).

options.text: A, B, C and D — and no further additions, since their external revenue of ₹850 lakh is 94.4% of ₹900 lakh
options.explanation: A, B pass revenue; C (25) and E fail assets/revenue but C passes result (≥15); D passes assets (150 ≥ 100); E fails all three (50 < 105, 12 < 15, 70 < 100). 850/900 ≥ 75% → stop.

options.text: A, B and D only
options.explanation: C's result of ₹25 lakh clears the ₹15 lakh result threshold.

questions.topic: Ch 4 · AS 20 · Case: Saraswati Textiles (EPS suite)
questions.case: Saraswati Textiles Ltd (year ended 31 March 20X2) had 2,40,000 equity shares on 1 April 20X1 and made a 1-for-4 bonus issue on 1 August 20X1. Net profit attributable to equity shareholders for 20X1-X2 is ₹12,00,000. For 20X0-X1 the company had reported EPS of ₹6.00 (on 2,40,000 shares). It also has ₹10,00,000 of 12% convertible debentures, convertible into 50,000 equity shares; the tax rate is 25%.

questions.stem: The weighted average number of equity shares for basic EPS of 20X1-X2 is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U5 — bonus issue in the denominator

options.explanation: Time-weights the bonus from 1 August (60,000 × 8/12) — bonus shares are never time-weighted.
Ignores the bonus issue entirely.
A bonus issue is treated as outstanding from the beginning of the year: 2,40,000 + 60,000, unweighted by the August date.
A half-year weighting of the bonus — no weighting applies at all.

questions.stem: Basic EPS for 20X1-X2 is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U5 — basic EPS

options.explanation: Divides by the pre-bonus 2,40,000 shares.
Divides by 2,80,000 — the wrongly time-weighted denominator.
Divides by 3,20,000 — check the bonus ratio: one for four on 2,40,000 is 60,000 shares.

questions.stem: The restated EPS for the comparative year 20X0-X1 is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U5 — restatement for bonus

options.explanation: 6.00 × 2,40,000/3,00,000 — the comparative is restated as if the bonus shares had always existed.
An arbitrary rounding; apply the share-ratio factor 240/300 exactly.
Leaving the comparative unrestated breaks comparability — AS 20 restates it.
Multiplies by 300/240 — the adjustment runs the other way (EPS falls when shares rise).

questions.stem: Diluted EPS for 20X1-X2 (nearest paisa) is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U5 — diluted EPS with convertible debt

options.explanation: Adds back gross interest ₹1,20,000 — the add-back is net of its 25% tax effect.
Omits the after-tax interest add-back of ₹90,000 to earnings.
Treats the debentures as anti-dilutive; 3.69 < 4.00, so they ARE dilutive and must be included.

questions.topic: Ch 4 · AS 25 · Case: Kaveri Foods (interim tax)
questions.case: Kaveri Foods Ltd publishes quarterly reports. For the year ending 31 March it estimates annual pre-tax income of ₹12,00,000, taxable at 30% on the first ₹6,00,000 and 40% on the balance. Actual pre-tax income of the first quarter (April–June) is ₹3,00,000. In October, the annual income estimate changes and the revised weighted average annual effective rate works out to 38%.

questions.stem: The weighted average annual effective income tax rate estimated at the first quarter is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U7 — weighted average annual rate

options.explanation: The first-slab rate alone ignores the ₹6,00,000 taxed at 40%.
Weights the slabs incorrectly — here the slabs happen to be equal, so the mean is 35%, not 32.5%.
The top-slab rate alone overtaxes the first ₹6,00,000.

questions.stem: Income tax expense recognised in the first quarter is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U7 — interim tax expense

options.explanation: 3,00,000 × 40% applies the marginal slab to the whole quarter.
3,00,000 × 38% uses the OCTOBER revision — Q1 used the rate estimated at Q1.
3,00,000 × 35% — the annual effective rate applies to each interim period's income.
3,00,000 × 30% uses the slab rate of the quarter's own income — slabs are annual, not quarterly.

questions.stem: When the estimated annual rate changes to 38% in the third quarter, AS 25 requires:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 4 U7 — changes in estimate across interim periods

options.text: Continuing at 35% until the annual statements true up
options.explanation: Later interim periods must move to the revised estimate as soon as it changes.

options.text: Recognising the entire catch-up in the annual statements only
options.explanation: The catch-up lands in the interim period of the change, not the year end.

options.text: Restating and reissuing the first-quarter report at 38%
options.explanation: Interim reports are not restated for changes in estimate.

options.text: Adjusting tax expense in subsequent interim periods so the year-to-date reflects 38% — with no restatement of the first quarter's report
options.explanation: Changes in estimate are absorbed prospectively within the year; earlier interim reports stand.

questions.stem: State the fundamental accounting assumptions under AS 1 and the related disclosure requirement. (4 marks)

skeleton.point: The three fundamental accounting assumptions: going concern, consistency and accrual
They are presumed to have been followed; no specific disclosure is required if followed
If any assumption is NOT followed, that fact must be disclosed
Purpose: users may otherwise assume compliance in reading the statements

questions.stem: What disclosures does AS 1 require when an enterprise changes an accounting policy? (4 marks)

skeleton.point: Any change with a material effect must be disclosed, with the amount of the effect to the extent ascertainable
Where the amount is not ascertainable wholly or in part, that fact must be disclosed
A change with no current material effect but one reasonably expected in later periods is disclosed in the period of the change
Disclosure never cures a wrong or inappropriate treatment

questions.stem: Classify the following into operating, investing and financing activities of a non-financial company, with reasons: (i) cash received from trade receivables; (ii) interest paid on debentures; (iii) dividend received on long-term investments; (iv) income tax paid; (v) proceeds of issue of equity shares. (5 marks)

skeleton.point: (i) Operating — collection from customers is the principal revenue-producing activity
(ii) Financing — interest paid is a cost of borrowing for a non-financial company
(iii) Investing — return on investments held, not a principal revenue activity
(iv) Operating — taxes on income unless specifically attributable to investing/financing
(v) Financing — changes the size and composition of owners' capital

questions.stem: Explain the tests for identifying reportable segments under AS 17, including the 75% rule. (5 marks)

skeleton.point: Revenue test: segment revenue (external + inter-segment) ≥ 10% of combined total revenue of all segments
Result test: |segment result| ≥ 10% of the greater in absolute amount of combined profit of profit-segments and combined loss of loss-segments
Asset test: segment assets ≥ 10% of total segment assets; ANY one test suffices
75% floor: if reportable segments' external revenue < 75% of enterprise revenue, add segments until reached
A previously reported segment of continuing significance stays reportable

questions.stem: Which parties are deemed NOT to be related under AS 18, and what disclosure exemptions does the standard provide? (4 marks)

skeleton.point: Deemed not related: companies with a mere common director (absent mutual policy influence); a single significant customer/supplier/franchiser/distributor/agent; providers of finance, trade unions, public utilities and government departments in normal dealings
Exemption where disclosure would conflict with confidentiality duties imposed by statute or regulator
State-controlled enterprises: no disclosure of transactions with other state-controlled enterprises
Intra-group transactions eliminated in consolidated financial statements need no disclosure there

questions.stem: Explain how a rights issue at less than fair value is dealt with in computing basic EPS, including the adjustment factor. (5 marks)

skeleton.point: A below-fair-value rights issue contains a bonus element (free value) inside a full-price issue
Shares outstanding before the issue (and comparatives) are multiplied by: fair value per share immediately before exercise ÷ theoretical ex-rights value per share
TERP = (aggregate fair value of shares immediately before exercise + rights proceeds) ÷ number of shares after exercise
From the exercise date the actual enlarged share count is weighted normally

questions.stem: Define a discontinuing operation under AS 24 and explain the initial disclosure event. (5 marks)

skeleton.point: Component being disposed of pursuant to a single plan — substantially in entirety (single transaction/demerger/spin-off), piecemeal, or by termination through abandonment
It must represent a separate major line of business or geographical area of operations
It must be distinguishable operationally and for financial reporting purposes
Initial disclosure event: EARLIER of a binding sale agreement for substantially all its assets, and board approval + announcement of a detailed formal plan

questions.stem: For a quarterly reporter, state the periods for which each interim statement is presented under AS 25. (4 marks)

skeleton.point: Balance sheet: at the current interim date, with a comparative at the close of the preceding full financial year
Profit and loss: the interim period itself PLUS cumulative year-to-date, each with comparatives for the matching periods one year back
Cash flow statement: cumulative year-to-date only, against the matching year-to-date of the preceding year
